content.text:
Ideals are to rings what normal subgroups are to groups. They allow us 
to construct quotient rings and are the kernels of ring homomorphisms.

content.title: Ideal
content.content:
A subset $I$ of a ring $R$ is an **ideal** if:

1. $(I, +)$ is a subgroup of $(R, +)$

2. For all $r \in R$ and $a \in I$: $ra \in I$ and $ar \in I$
We write $I \trianglelefteq R$.

content.text:
The second condition is called **absorption**. For commutative 
rings, we only need $ra \in I$ for all $r \in R, a \in I$.

content.title: Ideals in $\Z$
content.problem:
The ideals of $\Z$ are exactly $n\Z = \{nk : k \in \Z\}$ for 
$n \geq 0$. Every ideal of $\Z$ is principal (generated by one element).

content.title: Ideals in $\Z_n$
content.problem:
In $\Z_{12}$, the ideals are $\langle 1 \rangle = \Z_{12}$, 
$\langle 2 \rangle$, $\langle 3 \rangle$, $\langle 4 \rangle$, 
$\langle 6 \rangle$, and $\langle 0 \rangle = \{0\}$—exactly one for 
each divisor of $12$.

content.title: Principal Ideal
content.content:
An ideal $I$ of a commutative ring $R$ is **principal** 
if $I = (a) = aR = \{ar : r \in R\}$ for some $a \in R$.

content.title: Principal ideals in $\Z[x
content.problem:
$]

$(x) = \{xf(x) : f ℤ[x]\}$ consists of all polynomials with zero 
constant term.

$(2, x) = \{2f + xg : f, g ℤ[x]\}$ is **not** principal—it 
consists of polynomials with even constant term.

content.title: Operations on Ideals
content.content:
Let $I, J$ be ideals of $R$. Then:

1. $I \cap J$ is an ideal

2. $I + J = \{a + b : a \in I, b \in J\}$ is an ideal

3. $IJ = \{\sum a_i b_i : a_i \in I, b_i \in J\}$ is an ideal
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Prime Ideal
content.content:
A proper ideal $P$ of a commutative ring $R$ is **prime** 
if whenever $ab \in P$, either $a \in P$ or $b \in P$.

content.title: Maximal Ideal
content.content:
A proper ideal $M$ of $R$ is **maximal** if 
there is no ideal $I$ with $M \subsetneq I \subsetneq R$.

content.title: Prime and maximal ideals in $\Z$
content.problem:
In $\Z$, $(p)$ is prime iff $p$ is prime or $p = 0$. 
$(p)$ is maximal iff $p$ is prime. Note: $(0)$ is prime but not maximal.

items: Show that $\{0\}$ and $R$ are always ideals.

Show that every ideal of a field is either $\{0\}$ or the 
 entire field.

Prove that in a commutative ring with unity, every maximal 
 ideal is prime.

marginNotes.text:
And we know that in all things God works 
for the good of those who love him.
